lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: The sun doth gild our armour; up, my lords!

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Monte à cheval! My horse! varlet! laquais! ha!

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: O brave spirit!

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Via! les eaux et la terre.

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: Rien puis? l’air et le feu.

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Ciel, cousin Orleans.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Hark, how our steeds for present service neigh!

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Mount them, and make incision in their hides,

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: That their hot blood may spin in English eyes,

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: And dout them with superfluous courage, ha!

lines.speaker: RAMBURES
lines.text: What, will you have them weep our horses’ blood?

lines.speaker: RAMBURES
lines.text: How shall we, then, behold their natural tears?

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: The English are embattled, you French peers.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: To horse, you gallant princes! straight to horse!

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Do but behold yon poor and starved band,

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: And your fair show shall suck away their souls,

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Leaving them but the shales and husks of men.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: There is not work enough for all our hands;

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Scarce blood enough in all their sickly veins

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: To give each naked curtle-axe a stain,

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: That our French gallants shall today draw out,

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: And sheathe for lack of sport: let us but blow on them,

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: The vapour of our valour will o’erturn them.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: ’Tis positive ’gainst all exceptions, lords,

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: That our superfluous lackeys and our peasants,

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Who in unnecessary action swarm

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: About our squares of battle, were enough

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: To purge this field of such a hilding foe,

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Though we upon this mountain’s basis by

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Took stand for idle speculation:

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: But that our honours must not. What’s to say?

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: A very little little let us do,

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: And all is done. Then let the trumpets sound

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: The tucket sonance and the note to mount;

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: For our approach shall so much dare the field

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: That England shall crouch down in fear and yield.

lines.speaker: GRANDPRÉ
lines.text: Why do you stay so long, my lords of France?

lines.speaker: GRANDPRÉ
lines.text: Yond island carrions, desperate of their bones,

lines.speaker: GRANDPRÉ
lines.text: Ill-favouredly become the morning field:

lines.speaker: GRANDPRÉ
lines.text: Their ragged curtains poorly are let loose,

lines.speaker: GRANDPRÉ
lines.text: And our air shakes them passing scornfully:

lines.speaker: GRANDPRÉ
lines.text: Big Mars seems bankrupt in their beggar’d host

lines.speaker: GRANDPRÉ
lines.text: And faintly through a rusty beaver peeps:

lines.speaker: GRANDPRÉ
lines.text: The horsemen sit like fixed candlesticks,

lines.speaker: GRANDPRÉ
lines.text: With torch-staves in their hand; and their poor jades

lines.speaker: GRANDPRÉ
lines.text: Lob down their heads, drooping the hides and hips,

lines.speaker: GRANDPRÉ
lines.text: The gum down-roping from their pale-dead eyes,

lines.speaker: GRANDPRÉ
lines.text: And in their pale dull mouths the gimmal bit

lines.speaker: GRANDPRÉ
lines.text: Lies foul with chew’d grass, still and motionless;

lines.speaker: GRANDPRÉ
lines.text: And their executors, the knavish crows,

lines.speaker: GRANDPRÉ
lines.text: Fly o’er them, all impatient for their hour.

lines.speaker: GRANDPRÉ
lines.text: Description cannot suit itself in words

lines.speaker: GRANDPRÉ
lines.text: To demonstrate the life of such a battle

lines.speaker: GRANDPRÉ
lines.text: In life so lifeless as it shows itself.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: They have said their prayers, and they stay for death.

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Shall we go send them dinners and fresh suits

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: And give their fasting horses provender,

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: And after fight with them?

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: I stay but for my guidon: to the field!

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: I will the banner from a trumpet take,

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: And use it for my haste. Come, come, away!

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: The sun is high, and we outwear the day.

stage_directions.text: Enter the Dauphin, Orleans, Rambures, and others.

glosses.definition: gilds our armour — the sun turns the French armour golden
French: 'Mount on horse' — the Dauphin's cry for his mount
French: 'knave! lackey!' — the Dauphin calling servants
Italian/French exclamation — 'off we go', 'away'
French: 'the waters and the earth' — mock-elemental dialogue

glosses.phrase: Rien puis? l’air et le feu
glosses.definition: French: 'Nothing more? The air and fire' — Orleans adds the other two elements; the four classical elements ascribed playfully to their horses
French: 'Heaven, cousin Orleans' — the Dauphin tops him
cut the horses' hides — the Dauphin's grotesque fantasy of the horses weeping blood
extinguish (English eyes) with excess courage — 'dout' = do out, extinguish
Rambures's deflation — if the horses weep blood now, they have nothing left for natural grief
drawn up in battle array
the mere sight of the French army will drain English morale
empty shells and chaff — what will remain of the English
cutlass; short broad sword — 'curtle' = short
the breath of our courage — will blow the English over
surplus servants — French camp-followers, more than needed
the square formations of troops
worthless, cowardly foe — 'hilding' = good-for-nothing
mere watching — 'we wouldn't even have to fight, just observe'
a trumpet fanfare — 'tucket' = flourish, 'sonance' = sound
those English island-carcasses — 'carrion' = dead flesh
tattered banners
Mars, god of war, looks ruined — the English have no martial splendour
rust-stained visor — 'beaver' = the lower face-guard of a helmet
motionless candlesticks — the English riders don't move
torch-poles — their weapons look ornamental, not deadly
droop their heads heavily
mucus hanging in strings — sick horses
a jointed horse-bit with two rings
the crows as the horses' death-executors — already circling

glosses.phrase: send them dinners and fresh suits
glosses.definition: Dauphin's gibe — we might as well feed them before killing them
a small pennant carried by a cavalry unit
waste the daylight

annotations.title: Grandpré's mocking survey — English as dead meat
annotations.body: Lord Grandpré's speech describing the English army before battle is a small masterpiece of contemptuous portraiture. The English are 'island carrions' — dead flesh — 'desperate of their bones,' their tattered banners hanging limp, 'Big Mars seems bankrupt in their beggar'd host.' The horses 'Lob down their heads,' their bits fouled with chewed grass; the crows are the horses' 'executors,' impatient for the hour. It is a memorable image and (like 3.7) an intensifier of the battle's dramatic irony: the French look at this army and see food for carrion-birds. The English will win in a few hours. Notice also that Grandpré's contempt is specifically for the English army's ragged appearance, not for its fighting quality — he doesn't understand that the longbow will make appearance irrelevant. The scene closes with the Constable reaching for his guidon: the battle starts at the end of this speech.
sources: Juliet Barker, Agincourt: The King, the Campaign, the Battle (2005)
Emrys Jones, Scenic Form in Shakespeare (1971)

trials.prompt: Grandpré describes the English army as —
options: 'Island carrions, desperate of their bones' — dead meat, with crows already circling as their 'executors' — a contemptuous image that the battle is about to falsify

trials.prompt: What does the French attitude in 4.2 accomplish structurally?
options: It amplifies the dramatic irony of the coming victory: the French are certain, the audience knows Agincourt is minutes away, and the English (unseen) are already saying the prayer that 4.3 will stage
